concerns.text: The validation design contains a fundamental selection bias (confounding) that invalidates the comparison. FR-002 and US-02 specify that the 'Thermodynamic Model' is trained ONLY on entries with valid Materials Project (MP) data, while the 'Composition-Only Baseline' is trained on the FULL dataset (including entries where MP data is missing). This violates the independence of the comparison: any performance difference (R² gain) could be caused by the specific subset of alloys that have MP data (e.g., common commercial alloys) rather than the thermodynamic descriptors themselves. To isolate the feature effect, both models MUST be trained on the exact same subset of data (the intersection of available data).

The statistical test selection (Corrected Resampled t-test) assumes the two models are evaluated on the SAME data splits. However, the current design (FR-002) implies the models are evaluated on DIFFERENT datasets (Thermo on MP-subset, Baseline on Full). A paired statistical test on CV scores is mathematically invalid if the underlying samples (rows) differ between the two models. The design must ensure identical training/test splits for both models to make the paired test valid.

The stratification strategy ('10-fold stratification by temperature quantiles') is methodologically unsound for the stated goal of isolating 'composition vs. microstructure'. Creep rupture time is a function of Temperature AND Stress. Stratifying only by Temperature ignores the Stress variable, which is a primary driver of creep. If the Stress distribution varies significantly across folds, the model may learn Stress-temperature interactions rather than composition effects. Stratification should be based on the target variable (rupture time) or a combination of Temperature and Stress to ensure representative distribution of the response variable across folds.
